author: @buzzwerd8093
text:
The junction area gets you current. If each cell has 1 thin steel tube between hot and cold, copper (NiCu alloy is better) wire to go from one hot end to the next cold end and a piece of shim stock copper sheet curled into the tube end with the wire end under will expand against the steel and finish tightening contact with ID x length x pi area. Feed the output to a Joule Thief battery charger! Use solar energy in countries when the sun shines to charge cell phones? I have DC-DC boost converters that take 0.9V and give 5V at less current with 95% efficiency, see why loads of current is desired?
BTW the Thomson Effect explains where the current-resistance heat goes, straight to the cold junction as extra electron energy!

author: @fredepps3196
text: The use of a copper oxide junction reminds me of a patent I found a couple of years ago, US3516871, “method of producing electric current utilizing a copper oxide thermoelectric generator”. There are several unique things about this patent. The device is a small cylindrical copper casing .3 cm wide and .6 long filled with Copper oxide(s) made by melting a copper wire over the open end with an oxidizing flame. One electrode is attached to the case and the other is inserted part way down the center. When heated to 800 C the device produces 400 mA at .2 V. or 80 mW. Given the size of the device this is a fairly incredible power density. It’s the only time I’ve seen bulk CuO used in a TEG since most people have been inclined to oxidize wires for convenience.

text: I had attempted to make this style of thermo electric into a flue pipe, trying to keep the contact pressure adequate without grounding was frustrating. I eventually moved on to swaging copper pipe so that it sleeves inside the next. I also painted on a slightly thicker layer of copper oxide. Worked better, but still not great for the price. Thin copper flue pipe swaged in the same way would be the most ideal I think, but I've yet to find such a thing

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text:
My dad used to have one like this.  It was a series of rings of copper strips (alternating with some other metal IIRC), riveted into the same radial form you did.  Each strip was about 10 mm wide.  Then there were maybe 20 stacks of these one on top of the other - the stack was about 200 mm high.  A kerosene burner with a wick sat under it as the heat source up the middle of the stack, and the 'fins' of the cold ends were simply exposed to the colder air in the room. The device gave a good 12 Volts, and of course the waste heat warmed the room.   In the 1950s we used to sit in our living room in New Zealand and listen to the BBC shortwave broadcasts on a radio powered by this thing, while keeping warm at the same time.  T'was majik!  
Stick one in the focus of a small solar collector and you would be good to go.
Of course these are the same principle as used in NASA's radioisotope thermoelectric generators on rovers and deep space missions like Voyager.  
Just find a decent dT and you are all sorted!

author: @wesbaumguardner8829
text: You are going to have to find a dielectric that can handle some heat.  When you put that wood in your rocket stove, it is going to burn.  Furthermore, you may have issues creating current in the rocket stove because the whole stove is going to get real hot real fast and you will no longer have the temperature differential.  Thus you are going to have to have a way to have a portion of your wires outside of the rocket stove.  It is going to be difficult to do that without air leakage and grounding your wires so that the electricity just flows to the ground instead of to your load.  Neat idea, though.

author: @g0fvt
text:
I have an ancient copy of a book called "The Boy Electrician"" published in 1913, it details an almost identical assembly using copper wire and "german silver" wire which is in fact copper nickel wire.
The book suggests using 40 or 50 pieces of each type of wire and mentions an output of about 6v 250mA.(using 16swg wire and a spirit lamp) About the same voltage per junction as yours. Not a huge amount of power but quite enough to charge a mobile phone slowly or to power quite a bright LED. I have owned a copy of the book for well over 50 years now, since I was a kid, highly recommended reading for experimenters as there are a lot of ideas in it.

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: Look up thermocouple. That’s how it works. The oxide layer creates a barrier that causes the two pieces of copper to react as two separate metals.

replies.author: @PetraKann
replies.text: @mikebond6328 In fact the Peltier effect is a reverse thermocouple

replies.author: @poosmate
replies.text: I believe (please somebody correct me if I'm wrong), each strand of copper generates electric from its heated end to its cooled end so if you only had one line of copper you would only get one small output.  So you have to have lots.  And the bigger difference in the heat and cold the bigger the voltage or amps are.  There are better explanations out there, type in the words "Thermoelectric" and "Peltier"  or "TEG" (Thermo Electric Generators)into your search bar and browse other YT vids.  Take care, Poo

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
OK, so this is the DIY for absolutely anybody version, which I love. But for those wishing to push this further, Rob has a video on laser-sintering copper traces onto glass at home. 
I suspect one could laser-sinter copper tracesonto a heat-resistant ceramic, on both sides, and then oxidise the ends. So that when these "tiles" are stacked on top of each other, the traces overlap and form junctions. So now you have hundreds of junctions in a small form-factor and that is robust.

author: @scotttovey
text:
On older pilot lit, gas powered, hot water tanks and furnaces, they installed a thermo-cupler that was positioned so it was heated by the pilot light. The electricity generated in turn kept a gas valve open which supplied gas for the pilot light.

If the pilot light went out, or the thermo-cupler stopped producing electricity, the valve that fed the pilot light would be closed and prevent gas from building up.

It would be an interesting comparison between what you built and those things.

replies.author: @jeffstout6567
replies.text:
The residential type of equipment that used thermal couples are 25-35 millivolts. And commercial equipment such as restaurants and industry the commonly used 250 millivolts.  This is in the U.S. 
   when you can stack 2amps @  13 v dc worth of thermal  couples you would have a battery charger.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@jeffstout6567

Apart from that, a great experiment! I assume the heat is knocking electrons out of their band in a higher one, making them free to go.
I am not sure why you mentioned solar. Did you refer to anything of a solar application with this wire? I'd like to know that as well.

author: @CRHall-ud9mq
text:
Shy about asking, showing my lack of knowledge/understanding, but other than using like hair pins of copper wire, does thickness and/or alternative material (metals or minerals?) make a big difference as a solar cell(?)... And, would a battery and transformer be of any use in this?
 I've been puzzling over how valuable or not is the heat energy from thermally controlled composting; after all, it would generate 24/7.

replies.author: @jeffstout6567
replies.text: Biogas digester would be a consistent small flame for a long time!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It most certainly will mate - this is made from a copper/copper oxide junction and is just obtainable, easy to handle, cheap to experiment with and easy to make but other more exotic and hard to handle materials will do a better job that is for sure - basically heat Is the source of all energy but I think of heat and movement as the same thing - that means going a bit deeper into the physics I am afraid

author: @MrBillTroop73
